title: Felting with fine merino tops
subtitle: What changes when you switch from standard roving to 18-micron combed tops
excerpt: Fine merino tops (18 to 19 micron) felt faster and to a smoother, more refined surface than standard merino roving. This entry explains the key differences in working method: less agitation time, gentler rubbing, and finer surface detail possible.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional felting practice. The differences between standard roving and fine combed tops are documented in British felting guild teaching and in commercial fibre preparation literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed merino wool with the fibres parallel, sold as a flat batt or long ribbon. Tops are finer and more even in texture than carded roving, and felt more quickly.
The diameter of individual wool fibres measured in micrometres. Lower numbers mean finer, softer fibre. Standard felting merino is often 21 to 23 micron; fine merino tops are 18 to 19 micron.
A felting method using hot soapy water and agitation to lock wool fibres together into a dense, stable fabric.

content.text: felt faster than standard carded roving and make a smoother surface. The basic
method is the same. But the working time is shorter and rubbing needs to be gentler, or the surface compacts before the lower layers have joined.

Standard merino roving is usually 21 to 23 micron and has been carded, which means the fibres are more randomised and take slightly longer to lock together. Fine tops are combed to make the fibres parallel, which means each fibre is in contact with its neighbours from the start and the felting begins almost immediately on contact with hot water. Expect to cut your rubbing time by about a third compared with carded roving.

Because the fibres in tops are parallel, you need to lay the layers in alternating directions more carefully than with roving. Pull the tops into thin even wisps and lay each layer at 90 degrees to the previous. Skipping the alternating direction produces a piece that is stronger in one direction than the other and will stretch rather than stay flat in use.

Begin with a very gentle pressing-and-patting motion rather than rubbing straight away. Once the layers have started to knit together on the surface (about 3 minutes), switch to light circular rubbing. Avoid vigorous rubbing in the early stages: fine tops at high temperature can felt so fast on the surface that the lower layers are still loose, producing a patchy result.

The smooth, even surface of fine tops is the best base for needle-felted surface detail added after wet felting. The fine fibre also takes dye very evenly, making tops the preferred choice for projects where a precise, repeatable colour is important.

items.problem: The surface felted firm but the layers separated when I stretched the piece
items.cause: The alternating layer direction was not maintained, so the fibres in some layers are all parallel and do not interlock across layers
items.fix: Work back over the piece with more hot water and rubbing. The piece can usually be consolidated further with extended rubbing; the separation is a sign of under-fulling at depth rather than a surface issue.

items.problem: The piece over-felted in the first 10 minutes and is already too stiff to shape
items.cause: Fine tops felt very fast at high temperature. Hard rubbing in the first few minutes can compact the surface before the piece has shrunk to size.
items.fix: Start with cooler water (50 to 55 degrees Celsius rather than the hottest available) and reduce initial rubbing time to 5 minutes. Fine tops give more control at lower temperatures.
